title: 🌋 Sahel Droughts: How Climate Killed 100,000 in West Africa
description: Between 1968 and 1985, the Sahel region of West Africa experienced two devastating droughts. Lake Chad shrank by more than 90 percent. Crop yields collapsed. At least 100,000 people died of famine. Tens of millions were displaced. The drought reshaped global understanding of desertification, climate variability, and humanitarian response. #Sahel #Drought #WestAfrica #Desertification #DisasterDecode

segments.voice: Between 1968 and 1985, the Sahel region of West Africa — the semi-arid belt south of the Sahara — experienced two devastating multi-year droughts. Annual rainfall across the region dropped to half or less of historical averages. Crop yields collapsed. Pastures dried up. Livestock died in millions. Wells went dry. Lake Chad shrank from 25,000 square kilometers to less than 1,500. Approximately 100,000 to 200,000 people died of famine and famine-related disease, with some estimates running higher. Tens of millions were displaced. The Sahel droughts reshaped global understanding of climate variability, desertification, and humanitarian response.
segments.visual: Sahel droughts 1968 to 1985 West Africa Lake Chad shrinkage 100000 dead famine displacement

segments.voice: The Sahel runs across approximately ten countries: Senegal, Mauritania, Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad, Sudan, South Sudan, Eritrea, and Djibouti. The total area is approximately 3 million square kilometers. The region marks the transition between the Sahara to the north and the savanna to the south. Annual rainfall normally ranges from 100 millimeters in the north to 600 millimeters in the south. The agriculture and pastoralism of the region depends on a single rainy season concentrated in the months of June through September.
segments.visual: Sahel ten countries Senegal Mali Niger Chad Sudan 3 million square kilometers transition

segments.voice: Population of the Sahel countries in 1968 was approximately 25 million. The economies were predominantly rural and agricultural. Pastoralists — traditional cattle, camel, and goat herders — moved seasonally between northern and southern grazing lands. Sedentary farmers cultivated millet, sorghum, and groundnuts. Trans-Saharan trade routes connected the region to North Africa. The economies were vulnerable to weather variation by their structure. A single bad year could push communities to the edge of survival.
segments.visual: Sahel population 25 million pastoralists farmers trans-Saharan trade vulnerable weather

segments.voice: The 1968 to 1973 drought was the first phase. Rainfall dropped beginning in 1968 and continued declining through 1972. By 1973, the cumulative shortfall had become catastrophic. Rivers that pastoralists relied on dried up. Wells deepened by hundreds of meters in some areas to find water. Cattle herds that had been maintained for generations died in single dry seasons. Millions of livestock perished across Niger, Mali, and Mauritania. The pastoral economy of the region was effectively destroyed.
segments.visual: 1968 to 1973 first drought rainfall decline rivers wells cattle pastoral economy destroyed

segments.voice: Lake Chad, the regional water heart, contracted dramatically. The lake had been historically variable in size but generally remained between 20,000 and 25,000 square kilometers. By 1972, it had contracted to about 12,000. By 1985, it had reached approximately 1,500 square kilometers. Communities that had fished the lake for generations lost their livelihoods. The Lake Chad Basin Commission, an intergovernmental body created in 1964 by Nigeria, Niger, Chad, and Cameroon, found itself coordinating a steadily shrinking resource.
segments.visual: Lake Chad 25000 to 1500 square kilometers contraction Basin Commission 1964 four countries

segments.voice: The international response was initially slow. Drought is a slow-onset disaster. There is no single dramatic moment when intervention is obviously required. The cumulative deterioration of conditions in the Sahel between 1968 and 1973 was visible to local populations but largely invisible to international news coverage. Reporting from the region was limited. Most international audiences became aware of the disaster only in late 1973 and 1974, when famine images from Ethiopia, Mali, and Niger reached television networks.
segments.visual: international response slow onset disaster cumulative invisible reporting 1973 1974 images

segments.voice: Here's what nobody saw coming: the scale of the desertification debate that followed. Some scientists in the 1970s argued that the Sahara was advancing southward by several kilometers per year, driven by overgrazing, deforestation, and climate change. The narrative of an expanding desert became dominant in international media and policy circles. The United Nations Conference on Desertification, held in Nairobi in 1977, was a direct response. The Conference produced the Plan of Action to Combat Desertification, which guided much of subsequent decades of international assistance.
segments.visual: desertification narrative Sahara advancing UN Conference Nairobi 1977 Plan of Action

segments.voice: But subsequent satellite analysis has substantially revised the desertification narrative. Studies in the 1980s and 1990s, using long-term remote sensing data, showed that the Sahara-Sahel boundary was not advancing in a unidirectional way. The boundary fluctuated with rainfall variation. In drought years it appeared to advance; in wet years it retreated. The 1990s and 2000s saw substantial vegetation recovery in many parts of the Sahel. Some researchers now describe the region as greening rather than desertifying.
segments.visual: satellite analysis 1980s 1990s revision boundary fluctuates greening rather than desertify

segments.voice: The cause of the 1968 to 1985 drought is now better understood. Climate scientists have linked the drought to changes in sea surface temperatures across the Atlantic and Indian Oceans. The classical pattern of West African summer rainfall depends on contrasts between Atlantic ocean temperatures and the temperature of the Sahara. When those contrasts weaken — as they did during the 1970s and 1980s — the rainfall belt shifts southward and thins. Anthropogenic aerosols and natural decadal oscillations both contribute to such changes.
segments.visual: drought cause Atlantic Indian sea surface temperatures monsoon shift aerosols decadal

segments.voice: The death toll from the Sahel droughts is uncertain. Direct deaths from starvation are difficult to count in remote populations without robust civil registration. Indirect deaths from disease facilitated by malnutrition are harder still to attribute. Some estimates put the total at 100,000. Some range higher, suggesting 250,000 or more. The Ethiopian famine of 1984 to 1985, which is sometimes counted as part of the Sahel crisis and sometimes treated separately, killed approximately 1 million people on its own.
segments.visual: death toll uncertain 100000 to 250000 Ethiopian famine 1984 to 1985 1 million

segments.voice: The Ethiopian famine in particular became globally famous through the BBC's reporting from October 1984. Correspondent Michael Buerk's reports from Korem feeding camp showed thousands of starving children waiting for food that was not arriving. The footage triggered an unprecedented international response. Bob Geldof and Midge Ure organized Band Aid in November 1984. Live Aid, the global concert event, took place on July 13, 1985. The fundraising raised approximately 150 million dollars and drew sustained Western public attention to African famine for the first time.
segments.visual: BBC Michael Buerk Korem 1984 Band Aid Live Aid Geldof 150 million attention

segments.voice: But the tremor was just the start of the political analysis. The Ethiopian famine has been substantially attributed by many historians to the policies of the Derg regime under Mengistu Haile Mariam. Drought provided the trigger. Government policies of forced resettlement, military operations against insurgent regions, and grain price controls amplified the suffering. Aid that arrived was sometimes diverted to feed government soldiers. The lesson is that famines are rarely caused by drought alone — they emerge from the interaction of weather and politics.
segments.visual: Mengistu Derg regime forced resettlement military grain controls aid diverted weather politics

segments.voice: Amartya Sen, the Indian economist who later won the Nobel Prize in Economics in 1998, used the African famines to refine his theory of famine causation. Sen argued that famines occur not primarily because of food shortage but because of failures in entitlement — the legal and economic mechanisms by which people obtain food. A drought can destroy crops without causing famine if government and markets respond effectively. The Sahel and Ethiopian famines fit Sen's framework: drought triggered the crisis, but political and economic failure caused the deaths.
segments.visual: Amartya Sen Nobel 1998 entitlement theory framework drought political economic failure

segments.voice: Humanitarian response to the Sahel droughts shaped modern international aid. The United Nations created the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs in 1991 partly in response to the lessons of the 1970s and 1980s. The World Food Programme, established in 1961, was substantially expanded based on Sahel and Ethiopian crisis experience. The principle of early warning systems for slow-onset disasters became established practice. The Famine Early Warning Systems Network, run by the U.S. Agency for International Development, was created in 1985 specifically as a response.
segments.visual: humanitarian response UN OCHA 1991 World Food Programme FEWS NET 1985 USAID

segments.voice: The 1980s saw substantial migration out of the Sahel. Pastoralists who had lost their herds moved to cities. Urban populations of Bamako, Mali; Niamey, Niger; Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso; and N'Djamena, Chad approximately doubled between 1968 and 1985. Many migrants moved further, to coastal West African cities or to Europe. The Sahelian diaspora — Senegalese, Malian, and Burkinabé communities in France and other European countries — has its origins partly in the displacement of the drought years.
segments.visual: migration Sahel cities Bamako Niamey Ouagadougou N'Djamena diaspora France

segments.voice: Long-term changes in Sahel rainfall have been monitored extensively since the 1980s. The general pattern shows the dry phase of the 1970s and 1980s followed by a partial wetting in the 1990s and 2000s. Rainfall in the 2010s and 2020s has been variable but generally above 1980s levels. Whether this represents permanent recovery or a temporary swing in a longer cycle is debated. Climate models project varied outcomes for future Sahel rainfall, with some models showing continued recovery and others showing renewed decline.
segments.visual: rainfall monitoring 1980s recovery 1990s 2000s variability 2010s 2020s models projections

segments.voice: The Lake Chad crisis has continued. The lake remains far below its historical extent. The combined drought and water diversion for irrigation and human consumption has reduced the lake to roughly 10 percent of its 1960s size. Fishing communities around Lake Chad have collapsed. Pastoralists have moved further south in search of water. The Lake Chad Basin has become one of the most acute Sahelian humanitarian crisis zones, complicated by the rise of armed groups including Boko Haram in northeastern Nigeria.
segments.visual: Lake Chad 10 percent diversion irrigation collapsed pastoralists Boko Haram Nigeria

segments.voice: Boko Haram emerged in northeastern Nigeria in the 2000s and gained substantial territorial control by 2014. The insurgency has displaced approximately 3 million people across Nigeria, Niger, Cameroon, and Chad. Many of the displaced live in camps near Lake Chad and rely on humanitarian assistance. The combination of climate stress, economic collapse, weak governance, and armed conflict has produced one of Africa's most complex contemporary humanitarian crises. The drought of the 1970s and 1980s set the stage for much of what followed.
segments.visual: Boko Haram Nigeria 2000s 2014 displaced 3 million camps climate conflict complex

segments.voice: The Great Green Wall initiative, launched by the African Union in 2007, attempts to address Sahelian environmental degradation through tree planting. The plan envisions an 8,000-kilometer-long band of forests across the entire Sahel from Senegal to Djibouti. Implementation has been uneven. Some sections have shown success — particularly Senegal, where local communities have been actively engaged. Other sections have struggled with funding gaps, security challenges, and institutional weakness. Approximately 4 percent of the original target had been planted by 2020.
segments.visual: Great Green Wall 2007 8000 kilometers Senegal success funding security 4 percent

segments.voice: What does Sahel teach about climate disasters? The interaction between climate variability and political structures determines whether disasters become catastrophes. Agricultural and pastoral systems that have evolved with stable climate patterns are vulnerable to climate shifts that exceed historical variability. Population growth, infrastructure deficits, and political instability amplify climate impacts. Climate change in the future will create similar pressures across many regions. The Sahel is a model of what can go wrong when climate exceeds historical variation.
segments.visual: Sahel lesson climate political amplification population growth infrastructure future regions

segments.voice: Modern climate projections for the Sahel show substantial uncertainty. Some climate models project increased rainfall as the Atlantic warms and shifts pressure patterns. Others project continued drying as broader monsoon dynamics weaken. The disagreement among models reflects genuine scientific uncertainty about the dominant physical mechanisms. The lived consequence is that Sahelian populations cannot rely on stable climate planning. Investment in climate adaptation must hedge against multiple futures.
segments.visual: climate projections Sahel uncertainty models disagreement Atlantic monsoon adaptation

segments.voice: Historical analogues for the Sahel include the dust bowl of the United States Great Plains in the 1930s. The dust bowl displaced approximately 2.5 million people from Oklahoma, Kansas, and Texas. The Roosevelt administration responded with major federal programs including soil conservation, irrigation projects, and agricultural reform. The Sahel has not received comparable national-level investment in adaptation infrastructure. The dust bowl recovery in the United States took approximately a decade with significant federal capacity. Sahel recovery has been more uneven and prolonged.
segments.visual: Dust Bowl 1930s Oklahoma Kansas Texas Roosevelt federal investment recovery decade

segments.voice: Subscribe to DisasterDecode and hit the bell. Next week's disaster goes deeper into Pompeii — the Roman city buried by Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD, frozen in ash and ignored for 1,500 years.
segments.visual: Pompeii Vesuvius 79 AD Roman city buried ash 1500 years channel
